infobox.value: [South Central CT](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut")

is a [town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") in [New Haven County, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Haven_County,_Connecticut "New Haven County, Connecticut"), United States. The town's [nickname](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname") is "The Land of the [Sleeping Giant](/wiki/Sleeping_Giant_(Connecticut) "Sleeping Giant (Connecticut)")". The town is part of the [South Central Connecticut Planning Region](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut"). The population was 61,169 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[1]](#cite_note-Census_2020-1)

**Hamden** is a [town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") in [New Haven County, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Haven_County,_Connecticut "New Haven County, Connecticut"), United States. The town's [nickname](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname") is "The Land of the [Sleeping Giant](/wiki/Sleeping_Giant_(Connecticut) "Sleeping Giant (Connecticut)")". The town is part of the [South Central Connecticut Planning Region](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut"). The population was 61,169 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[1]](#cite_note-Census_2020-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hamden,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The peaceful [[2]](#cite_note-2) tribe of Quinnipiacs were the first residents of the land that is now Hamden; they had great regard, awe and veneration for the Blue Hills Sleeping Giant Mountain.

In spring 1638, [Theophilus Eaton](/wiki/Theophilus_Eaton "Theophilus Eaton") and the Reverend [John Davenport](/wiki/John_Davenport_(Puritan) "John Davenport (Puritan)") purchased, from [Quinnipiac](/wiki/Quinnipiac "Quinnipiac") Chief Momauguin, the land that would become the settlement of New Haven Colony.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Later that November, Eaton expanded the settlement by acquiring 130 square miles from [Mattabesset](/wiki/Mattabesset "Mattabesset") Chief Montowese. This tract extended the original settlement 10 miles north along the [Quinnipiac River](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River "Quinnipiac River"), with an additional 8 miles to the east and 5 mi. to the west. This piece of land included what would eventually become Hamden. The payment was made with "11 coats of trucking cloth and a fine coat for the chief."[[4]](#cite_note-4)
It was settled by [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") as part of the town of [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut").

It remained a part of New Haven until 1786 when 1,400 local residents incorporated the area as a separate town, naming it after the English statesman [John Hampden](/wiki/John_Hampden "John Hampden").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Largely developed as a nodal collection of village-like settlements (which remain distinct today), including [Mount Carmel](/wiki/Mount_Carmel,_Hamden "Mount Carmel, Hamden") (home to [Quinnipiac University](/wiki/Quinnipiac_University "Quinnipiac University")), [Whitneyville](/wiki/Whitneyville,_Connecticut "Whitneyville, Connecticut"), Spring Glen, West Woods, and [Highwood](/wiki/Highwood,_Hamden "Highwood, Hamden"), Hamden has a long-standing industrial history. In 1798, four years after [Eli Whitney](/wiki/Eli_Whitney "Eli Whitney") began manufacturing the [cotton gin](/wiki/Cotton_gin "Cotton gin") in New Haven, he made arms for the U.S. government at a [mill](/wiki/Watermill "Watermill") site in Hamden, where a waterfall provided a good source of power. At that site, Whitney introduced the modern era of [mass production](/wiki/Mass_production "Mass production") with the concept of interchangeable parts.

The major thoroughfare through Hamden is named Whitney Avenue in honor of Eli Whitney, and it runs past Whitney's old factory, now the [Eli Whitney Museum](/wiki/Eli_Whitney_Museum "Eli Whitney Museum").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Eli_Whitney_Gun_Factory_William_Giles_Munson_1827.jpg/250px-Eli_Whitney_Gun_Factory_William_Giles_Munson_1827.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eli_Whitney_Gun_Factory_William_Giles_Munson_1827.jpg)

An 1827 painting of Whitneyville by William Giles Munson.

Whitney constructed stone houses for his employees in the nearby area, which is still referred to as Whitneyville; this is believed to be the first example of employer-provided homes in U.S. history. In 1806, the dam that Eli Whitney built at the mill site was enlarged to create a [reservoir](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir"), [Lake Whitney](/wiki/Lake_Whitney_(Connecticut) "Lake Whitney (Connecticut)"). The first [truss bridge](/wiki/Truss_bridge "Truss bridge") in the United States was erected nearby over the [Mill River](/wiki/Mill_River_(Connecticut) "Mill River (Connecticut)") in Whitneyville in 1823, but has since been replaced.

The [Farmington Canal](/wiki/Farmington_Canal "Farmington Canal"), which boats traveled from New Haven northward, passed through Hamden between 1825 and 1848 until it was supplanted by [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad") travel. The canal right-of-way has become, in recent years, a popular walking and bicycling trail, passing by some of the well-preserved [locks](/wiki/Lock_(water_transport) "Lock (water transport)") of the canal, as well as some of Hamden's oldest sites. Before its use as a walking and bicycling trail, many local residents rode their motocross bikes on the Farmington Canal.

During the 19th and early 20th centuries, Hamden received a steady influx of immigrants, most notably from [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). To this day, a large part of Greater New Haven's [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American") community resides in Hamden.

During the post-war period, Hamden underwent significant suburban development. Much of the southern section of town is urbanized and is difficult to distinguish from neighboring New Haven. The northern section of town, however, retains a more rural character, and has the distinct neighborhood of Mount Carmel. This area of town is the location of the unique [Sleeping Giant](/wiki/Sleeping_Giant_(Connecticut) "Sleeping Giant (Connecticut)") hill formation that is the source of the town's nickname.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hamden,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Hamden-CT-SleepingGiantMtn.jpg/250px-Hamden-CT-SleepingGiantMtn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hamden-CT-SleepingGiantMtn.jpg)

Hamden Connecticut's Sleeping Giant Mountain from the Quinnipiac river.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 33.3 square miles (86 km2), of which 32.8 square miles (85 km2) is land and 0.5 square miles (1.3 km2), or 1.62%, is water. The town features the [Mill River](/wiki/Mill_River_(Connecticut) "Mill River (Connecticut)"), which runs from the northern part of town, is dammed to form [Lake Whitney](/wiki/Lake_Whitney_(Connecticut) "Lake Whitney (Connecticut)"), and flows from there to [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"). The town also has the [Quinnipiac River](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River "Quinnipiac River") and Lake Wintergreen, as well as numerous small streams.

### Notable areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hamden,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Notable areas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Edgerton1.jpg/250px-Edgerton1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edgerton1.jpg)

[Edgerton Park](/wiki/Edgerton_Park "Edgerton Park") Conservancy

The Town Hall at the center of Hamden has a distinctive appearance. The rotunda includes commemorative stained glass windows. Across the street is Freedom Park, which contains a fountain with concrete stepping stones leading to a sign that pleads for peace in several different languages. The Town recently completed new facilities for the police and fire departments in the newly renovated Town Hall.

Hamden was host to the Ghost Parking Lot, a notable roadside [public art](/wiki/Public_art "Public art") [installation](/wiki/Installation_art "Installation art") by [James Wines](/wiki/James_Wines "James Wines") located in front of the Hamden Plaza [shopping center](/wiki/Shopping_center "Shopping center") in Hamden's commercial district on Dixwell Avenue. Erected in 1978, it consisted of 15 car hulks, specially treated and encased in asphalt. Although featured in over 100 art books, the attraction was torn down in 2003 due to the excessive cost of restoration and repair.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hamden,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Government")]

Hamden is governed by a [mayor-council](/wiki/Mayor-council "Mayor-council") form of government, with a 15-member legislative council. Six councilors are elected at large while the other nine are elected by district. Town elections are held biennially during odd years in November. Other elected positions in the town government are the Town Clerk and members of the Board of Education. Positions in the various town boards and commissions are generally appointed by the Mayor subject to approval by the Legislative Council.

Adam Sendroff became the town's 15th mayor in November 2025.[[12]](#cite_note-WTNH-12) Past mayors of Hamden are:

* Lauren Garrett, 2021-2025[[13]](#cite_note-New_Haven_Independent-13)
* Curt Balzano Leng, 2015–2021
* Scott Jackson, 2009–2015
* Craig Henrici, 2005–2009
* Carl Amento, 1999–2005
* Barbara DeNicola, 1997–1999
* Lillian Clayman, 1991–1997
* Johnny Carusone, 1987–1991
* John DeNicola Jr., 1985–1987
* Peter Villano, 1981–1985
* Dick Harris, 1979–1981
* Lucien DiMeo, 1973–1979
* Bill Adams, 1967–1973
* John DeNicola Sr., 1965–1967

John DeNicola Sr. was the town's last first selectman before becoming the town’s first mayor, in November 1965.

The main industries in the town are retail trade, computer products, manufacture of wire and cable, concrete, pump mixer products, fabricated metals, construction and business services. Business services account for 49.4% of employment in the town, with retail trade accounting for 22.8%, and manufacturing accounting for 9.6%. The top four major employers are the Town government and school district, [Quinnipiac University](/wiki/Quinnipiac_University "Quinnipiac University"), Harborside Health Care, and Area Cooperative Education Services (ACES).

[Shaw's Supermarket](/wiki/Shaw%27s_Supermarket "Shaw's Supermarket") was one of the top five major employers, but the Hamden Shaw's was sold to ShopRite in a sale of Shaw's Connecticut stores announced on February 13, 2010.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The Shaw's supermarket has been shut down and the new ShopRite store has opened.

Hamden is a residential suburb for New Haven, with more residents commuting to work in New Haven than residents working in Hamden.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hamden,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transportation")]

The [Wilbur Cross Parkway](/wiki/Wilbur_Cross_Parkway "Wilbur Cross Parkway") runs through the center of the town serving as a connection to [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut") to the north and the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") to the south. The town is connected to [Interstate 91](/wiki/Interstate_91_in_Connecticut "Interstate 91 in Connecticut") via [Connecticut Route 40](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_40 "Connecticut Route 40"), a spur expressway to the Mount Carmel section of town. The main route from the town center to New Haven passing through the commercial areas of Hamden is Dixwell Avenue ([Route 10](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_10 "Connecticut Route 10")). Another route to New Haven via the Spring Glen and Whitneyville residential neighborhoods is Whitney Avenue.

Public transportation is provided by [Connecticut Transit New Haven](/wiki/Connecticut_Transit_New_Haven "Connecticut Transit New Haven"). The main bus routes in the town are the Dixwell Avenue (238, formerly D) and the Whitney Avenue (228/229, formerly J) routes. Other secondary routes serving the town are the State Street (224, formerly M), Winchester Avenue (234, formerly O), and Shelton Avenue (237, formerly G) bus routes.

[North Haven Station](/wiki/North_Haven_(CDOT_station) "North Haven (CDOT station)"), a station on the [Hartford Line](/wiki/Hartford_Line "Hartford Line") [commuter railroad](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail"), is planned be built next to the Hamden–North Haven border near the Route 40 Connector, serving both towns.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Tweed New Haven Airport](/wiki/Tweed_New_Haven_Airport "Tweed New Haven Airport") ([HVN](/wiki/Tweed_New_Haven_Airport "Tweed New Haven Airport")) in [East Haven](/wiki/East_Haven,_Connecticut "East Haven, Connecticut") and [Bradley International Airport](/wiki/Bradley_International_Airport "Bradley International Airport") ([BDL](/wiki/Bradley_International_Airport "Bradley International Airport")) in [Windsor Locks](/wiki/Windsor_Locks,_Connecticut "Windsor Locks, Connecticut") are the closest commercial airports to Hamden.

## Quality of life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hamden,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Quality of life")]

Within the town limits, there are 16 banks, six lodging facilities, and 29 day care facilities. There are no hospitals in the town, although it is close to the major hospitals in New Haven. In 2004, the crime rate was 2,084 per 100,000 residents, lower than the statewide average of 2,981 per 100,000 residents. The town library has 166,358 volumes (as of 2001).

Electricity in the town is provided by the [United Illuminating](/wiki/United_Illuminating "United Illuminating") company; natural gas is provided by the [Southern Connecticut Gas](/wiki/Southern_Connecticut_Gas "Southern Connecticut Gas") company; the water provider is the [South Central Connecticut Regional Water Authority](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Regional_Water_Authority "South Central Connecticut Regional Water Authority"); Cable TV is provided by [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast") of New Haven.

There are several parks and museums located in Hamden. Hamden Town Center Park hosts fireworks, free concerts, outdoor movies, and other seasonal festivals. Other parks include the [Eli Whitney Museum](/wiki/Eli_Whitney_Museum "Eli Whitney Museum") in Whitneyville, [Ireland’s Great Hunger Museum](/wiki/Ireland%E2%80%99s_Great_Hunger_Museum "Ireland’s Great Hunger Museum"), parts of [West Rock Ridge State Park](/wiki/West_Rock_Ridge_State_Park "West Rock Ridge State Park") (including Lake Wintergreen) and [East Rock Park](/wiki/East_Rock_Park "East Rock Park") (including the Pardee Rose Garden), Brooksvale Park and the adjoining Mount Sanford block of [Naugatuck State Forest](/wiki/Naugatuck_State_Forest "Naugatuck State Forest"), and the [Sleeping Giant State Park](/wiki/Sleeping_Giant_State_Park "Sleeping Giant State Park"). The [Farmington Canal Trail](/wiki/Farmington_Canal_Trail "Farmington Canal Trail") runs through the town. Two blue-blazed hiking trails, the [Quinnipiac Trail](/wiki/Quinnipiac_Trail "Quinnipiac Trail") and the [Regicides Trail](/wiki/Regicides_Trail "Regicides Trail"), also run through the town.

The Jonathan Dickerman House, listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"), is located in Mount Carmel. Hamden also has an all-volunteer orchestra, the Hamden Symphony Orchestra, providing concerts throughout the year.

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[25]](#cite_note-GR2-25) of 2010, there were 60,690 people, 23,727 households, and 14,300 families residing in the town. The population density was 1,867.3 inhabitants per square mile (721.0/km2). There were 25,114 housing units at an average density of 769.2 per square mile (297.0/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 68.45% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 20.19% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.15% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 5.47% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.03% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 3.00% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 2.72% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 8.74% of the population.

There were 23,727 households, out of which 25.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 44.4% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 12.5% had a female householder with no husband present, and 39.7% were non-families. 31.2% of all households were made up of individuals, and 12.7% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.37 and the average family size was 3.01.

In the town, the population was spread out, with 24.4% under the age of 20, 10.6% from 20 to 24, 24.6% from 25 to 44, 25.4% from 45 to 64, and 15.0% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 37.4 years. For every 100 females, there were 82.6 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 80.7 males.

The median income for a household in the town was $66,695, and the median income for a family was $88,613. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $34,596. About 3.8% of families and 6.8% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 6.3% of those under age 18 and 5.4% of those age 65 or over.
